summary: Gruber argues that Apple has fundamentally reversed its historical pricing disadvantage. While Macs still cost more than typical Windows PCs, Apple's consumer electronics — iPods, iPhones, and iPads — are priced comparably to or lower than competitors. He attributes this to Apple's volume purchasing power (especially in flash storage), operational efficiency under Tim Cook, and the fact that consumer electronics competitors must match Apple's design quality, unlike PC makers who could always compete on cheap specs alone. The mobile computing market resembles consumer electronics more than the PC industry, which plays to Apple's strengths.
key_points: Unsubsidized iPhone prices are comparable to or lower than equivalent Android phones; lower subsidized Android prices reflect carriers playing handset makers against each other, not real cost advantages
No competitor has produced an iPod Touch equivalent — a high-end phone-like device without the phone contract — suggesting no one else can afford to at that price point
Apple's volume purchasing of flash storage and components, plus Tim Cook's operational efficiency, give Apple a structural cost advantage
The consumer electronics market forces competitors to match Apple's design quality, unlike the PC market where manufacturers competed on specs with inferior build quality
Microsoft's PlaysForSure strategy proved that the PC industry's commodity playbook doesn't work in consumer electronics
money_quotes: Today's Apple has turned the pricing story on its head. Used to be the knock against Apple was their kit was overpriced. Now, even putting quality aside, competitors can't match Apple's prices.

I would love to buy a $229 Android device that's the equivalent of the iPod Touch — i.e., something pretty much just like a high-end Android phone but without the phone (and without the contract). Three years and 30 or 40 million iPod Touch sales later, there remains no such Android device.
I suspect the main reason is that no other handset maker can afford to make one.
A carrier that wants the iPhone has one option: negotiate with Apple. A carrier that wants some good Android handsets can play HTC, Samsung, LG, Motorola, and whoever else against each other.
An awful lot of PCs have been sold to people who never even looked at the enclosure — only the specs. That's not how the game is played in consumer electronics.
Nobody knows what kind of CPU they have in their phones. (Where by 'nobody', I mean 'no regular people.')
The nascent mobile computing market has much more in common with the traditional consumer electronics market than it does with the PC industry, and that works very much in Apple's favor.
key_insight: Apple's consumer electronics pricing advantage stems from the fact that the mobile market rewards design and operational efficiency rather than spec-sheet commodity competition, reversing the dynamics that historically made Apple the expensive option.
